a_1.text:
Dawn breaks grey over the Atlantic, and there is Nassau — low and green against the pale sky, smoke rising from a dozen points along the harbour. You stand at the Adamant's bow rail, salt crusting your coat, and count the shapes on the horizon.

Three warships. Sitting broadside to the harbour mouth in a loose crescent. The closest — a sixty-gun ship of the line — flies the commodore's pennant. Your glass picks out the name on her stern: Crown's Justice. The two flanking her are smaller frigates. Fervent to the north. Resolve to the south.

Eleven days, the harbour pilot says as his boat bumps alongside. Eleven days and not a shot fired. He is a thin man with nervous hands. He looks at the Adamant the way a doctor looks at a patient who has walked into a plague ward voluntarily.

"Captain Morrow's holding things together," he says. "Barely. You picked a hell of a time to make port."

choices.text: Tell the pilot your business is your own
Ask the pilot what Morrow is doing about the blockade
Share your ship's condition openly — let your crew hear you're not hiding anything

a_1a.text:
The pilot shrugs. He has ferried enough captains to know when one is done talking. He guides you through the channel in silence, marking the shoals with quick gestures.

Pell stands at the helm, watching the blockade ships grow smaller as you slip past. "They let us through," he says quietly. "Didn't even hail us."

That is perhaps the most unsettling part. Ashford — whoever he is — is not stopping ships from entering Nassau. He is stopping them from leaving.

choices.text: Dock the Adamant

a_1b.text:
The pilot glances over his shoulder, as though the warships might hear him. "Morrow's got four ships that could fight. Maybe five if you count the Dorado, which you shouldn't — her hull's got more patch than plank. She's been holding parleys with the other captains. Keeping people calm."

He pauses. "Calm enough, anyway. There was a fight at the Broken Wheel three nights ago. Two men dead over a rumour about surrender terms. Morrow had the instigator flogged on the dock in front of everyone. That bought some quiet."

Pell catches your eye from the helm. He has been listening. He says nothing, but you know the look. He is measuring this place already.

choices.text: Dock the Adamant

a_1c.text:
You raise your voice enough for the crew to hear. The Adamant took some weather damage rounding the Caicos passage — nothing fatal, but the foremast needs stepping and the water casks are low. You tell the pilot this plainly, and you see your crew register it. No secrets. No posturing.

Pell nods from the helm. It is a small thing, but the crew's shoulders loosen half an inch.

The pilot guides you through the channel. The blockade ships sit on the horizon like headstones.

choices.text: Dock the Adamant

a_2.text:
The Adamant settles against the dock with a groan of hemp and timber. Nassau's harbour is a half-circle of rotting wharves and beached hulls, overlooked by the fort on the hill — a squat, ugly thing with cannon muzzles showing through every embrasure. The market runs along the waterfront: sail canvas and ship's biscuit, stolen silverware laid out on blankets, a woman selling oranges from a handcart.

The air is heavy. Tar, rotting fish, woodsmoke, and something sweeter underneath — rum or sugar or both. Ships line the harbour with their rigging stripped for sale. Men sit on coiled rope and watch you tie up. No one offers to help.

Pell steps onto the dock and turns a slow circle. "Cheerful place," he says.

choices.text: Ask Pell for his honest read of the situation
Secure the ship and head straight for the tavern
Look for the Harbour Council's office
Walk the docks and talk to the locals first
Walk through the market first
Climb the hill to the fort

a_2a.text:
Pell leans against a bollard and folds his arms. He has sailed with you long enough to speak plainly.

"Three warships and no shots fired means Ashford wants something specific. If he wanted to burn this place, he'd have done it already. That blockade is a negotiating position." He spits into the harbour. "Which means someone in this port has something he wants, and they haven't given it up yet."

He looks at you. "We've got maybe a week of provisions if we're careful. Two if the crew doesn't mind getting thin. Whatever you're planning, Captain — plan it quick."

From the far end of the dock, a man in a long coat watches the Adamant. He is too well-dressed for the waterfront, and he is not pretending otherwise.

choices.text: Head to the tavern — the Broken Wheel
Approach the well-dressed man
Walk the docks and ask around
Let Pell go ashore for supplies — it'll cost time but the crew needs it

a_2b.text:
Pell looks surprised. Not at the order — at the fact you thought to give it. "Aye, Captain. I'll take Tomas and see what the market's got. Fresh water at least. Maybe fruit if the price isn't robbery."

He hesitates. "You sure? Means you're going into town alone. First day in a place like this."

You tell him you'll manage. He nods and calls for Tomas. The crew watches this exchange with quiet attention. Their captain, sending the first mate to look after them instead of dragging everyone into whatever political mess is waiting onshore.

It is noted.

choices.text: Head to the Broken Wheel
Walk the docks first

a_3.text:
The Broken Wheel sits at the top of the harbour road — a two-storey building with a collapsed balcony on one side and a door that has been kicked in and rehung so many times it no longer sits true in the frame. Inside, the air is thick with pipe smoke and the sour smell of small beer.

Three people catch your attention.

At the bar, a woman with a naval tattoo on her forearm drinks alone. She carries a pistol openly and watches the room with professional attention. A Morrow lieutenant, by the look of her.

At a corner table, a man in a pressed linen coat writes figures in a ledger. His clothes are too clean for this place. He has an inkwell that probably cost more than most of the furniture. Aldous Crane — the Harbour Council's factor.

And at a table near the hearth, an old sailor with white hair and broken teeth nurses a cup of rum. He talks to no one, but his eyes are sharp.

choices.text: Approach the Morrow lieutenant
Approach Aldous Crane
Buy the old sailor a drink
Sit down and observe the room first

a_3a.text:
You take a seat where the wall meets the bar and order whatever they're pouring. It turns out to be rum cut with something that might charitably be called water. You drink it and watch.

The lieutenant checks the door every thirty seconds. She is not waiting for a friend. She is waiting for trouble. Whatever Morrow has her doing here, it involves keeping eyes on new arrivals.

Crane writes steadily, pausing only to tap his pen against the inkwell's rim — a precise, habitual gesture. He has noticed you. He noticed you the moment you walked in. He is simply choosing not to acknowledge it yet.

The old sailor mutters something to his cup. You catch the word "Cristóbal" and then he is quiet again.

choices.text: Approach the Morrow lieutenant
Approach Aldous Crane
Buy the old sailor a drink
Step outside — check the docks

a_10.text:
You cross the room and set your cup on the bar beside hers. She looks at you the way a cat looks at a dog that has wandered into the wrong yard.

"New ship. The sloop." It is not a question. "I'm Harlow. I sail with Captain Morrow." She does not offer her hand. "What brings you to Nassau, Captain? And I'll know if you're lying — I've heard every line there is."

choices.text: Tell her the truth — you're looking for opportunity and heard Nassau needed ships
Tell her you're here to trade and resupply — nothing more
Ask her directly for a meeting with Morrow

a_11.text:
Harlow studies you for a long moment. Then she picks up her drink and takes a slow pull.

"Opportunity." She says the word like she's turning it over in her hands, checking for splinters. "There's plenty of that here, if you don't mind the kind that gets you killed."

She sets her cup down. "Morrow doesn't meet with every captain who washes up. But she's practical. If you've got something to offer — a ship, a crew, a brain that works — she might make time." A pause. "Do something useful first. Prove you're not just another mouth to feed."

"There's a Crown marine been watching the harbour. Plainclothes, but he's got the boots and the posture. Morrow wants to know who sent him and why. Find that out, and I'll arrange a sit-down."

choices.text: Agree to investigate the marine
Ask what else Morrow needs — you'd rather choose your task

a_12.text:
Harlow's expression does not change, which is worse than if she had laughed. "Trade and resupply. With three warships blocking the harbour mouth." She takes a drink. "Sure."

She lets the silence sit for a moment. Then: "Look, Captain. Everyone who comes here right now has a reason that isn't trade. Either you're running from something, or you think there's something here worth running toward. Either way, Morrow will want to know about you eventually."

She taps the bar twice. "When you're ready to have a real conversation, you know where to find me."

choices.text: Push back — tell her you don't answer to Morrow
Concede the point and ask how to meet Morrow
Leave her and try Aldous Crane instead
Buy the old sailor a drink

a_13.text:
"A meeting." Harlow looks amused — which on her face means one corner of her mouth moves approximately two millimetres. "Morrow doesn't hold court. She's not a governor. But she keeps track of who's useful and who's dead weight."

She finishes her drink and sets the cup down with a click. "There's a Crown man skulking around the docks. Plainclothes — thinks nobody's noticed. Morrow wants to know who he reports to and what he's watching for. Handle that, and I'll bring you to her."

She pushes away from the bar. "Or don't. Plenty of captains in this harbour already."

choices.text: Agree — head to the docks to find the marine
Ask Harlow what Morrow thinks of the blockade

a_13a.text:
"Nobody answers to Morrow," Harlow says evenly. "That's the point. She's not Crown. She doesn't issue orders to captains she doesn't sail with." She turns to face you fully. "But this is her harbour. Her network. Her intelligence. And right now, her blockade to break. You want to operate here, you work with the people who keep the place running."

Her eyes are steady. "Or you sit on your sloop and wait for Ashford to starve you out with the rest of us. Your call."

The room has gone slightly quieter. Two men at a nearby table have stopped pretending not to listen.

choices.text: Back down — ask how to meet Morrow properly
Walk away and try a different approach

a_14.text:
Harlow considers you. "The blockade is Ashford's doing. Commodore Ashford, Crown's Atlantic Fleet. Three ships — you've seen them. He sailed in eleven days ago and dropped anchor. No demands. No ultimatum. He's waiting."

She lowers her voice. "Morrow thinks he's waiting for something specific. Not surrender — he could force that with a bombardment. Something in this port, or near it, that he can't get by burning the place down."

She glances at the old sailor by the hearth. A quick look, barely a flicker. Then back to you. "Handle the marine on the docks. That's the door to Morrow. Take it or leave it."

choices.text: Head to the docks to find the marine
Go talk to the old sailor Harlow glanced at

a_15.text:
Harlow is waiting at the Broken Wheel when you return. She listens to your report without interruption — a professional habit. When you finish, she nods once.

"So Ashford's watching new arrivals. That's useful. That's very useful." She stands and adjusts her pistol belt. "Come with me. Morrow will want to hear this herself."

She leads you up the harbour road, past the market, to a house set back from the waterfront. It is unremarkable from the outside — whitewashed stone, a garden gone to seed. Two armed men sit on the porch, cleaning their weapons with the unhurried ease of people who have been doing this for days.

choices.text: Follow Harlow inside

a_16.text:
The house is cooler inside. Maps on every wall. A table covered in charts and weighted down with pistol balls used as paperweights. The shutters are half-closed against the heat.

Reyna Morrow sits at the table with a cup of coffee — real coffee, which means she has supply lines that still function. She is younger than you expected. Late thirties, maybe. Dark hair cut short. A scar across her left hand that she does not hide. She wears no jewellery, no captain's coat, nothing performative.

She looks up when you enter. Her eyes take in everything — your weapons, your posture, the state of your boots — in about two seconds.

"The new sloop," she says. "Sit down. Tell me what you found on the docks."

choices.text: Report everything — the marine, his purpose, Ashford watching new arrivals
Report the basics but hold back details — keep some leverage

a_17.text:
You lay it out plainly. The marine. His orders. Ashford's interest in new ships entering the harbour. Morrow listens with her hands folded on the table. She does not interrupt. She does not react. When you finish, she is quiet for a moment.

"Good." She picks up her coffee. "You're thorough. I was told you were, but I don't trust what I'm told." She drinks. "Ashford is not a fool. He's watching this port because he knows something is here. Something he can't simply take by force."

She sets the cup down and meets your eyes. "I know what it is. I have known for three years. And I have not acted on it, for reasons that are mine."

The room is very quiet. Harlow stands by the door, watching.

choices.text: Ask her directly what Ashford is looking for
Tell her you've heard rumours about a wreck — the San Cristóbal
Ask what Ashford is really after

a_17a.text:
You give her the outline — a Crown marine watching the docks, plainclothes, interested in new arrivals. You leave out the specifics of what he said. Keep something in reserve.

Morrow watches you as you speak. When you finish, she tilts her head slightly. "That's not everything." It is not a question. "You're holding something back because you think it gives you leverage."

She stands and walks to the window. "I don't blame you. But understand — I have been doing this longer than you, in this harbour, with these people. Whatever you're holding back, I will find out on my own within a day. The only question is whether you were the one who told me."

Her back is to you. Harlow's hand rests on her pistol, though her expression has not changed.

choices.text: Give her the rest — full disclosure
Hold your ground — information is currency

a_18.text:
Morrow turns from the window. "What he is looking for is a ship. The San Cristóbal — a Crown galleon that went down in the Shallows of Perdition ten years ago. She was carrying a war chest. Gold, silver, standard Crown treasury consignment." She pauses. "And a sealed diplomatic pouch. Something called the Red Correspondence."

She sits back down. "The gold would fund this Republic for a year. But the pouch is what matters. Whatever is in it, Ashford has spent a decade making sure no one finds it. And now he's sitting three warships on our harbour because he thinks someone finally knows where the wreck is."

She looks at you steadily. "He's right. I do."

choices.text: Ask why she hasn't acted in three years
Offer your ship — the Adamant can dive the wreck

a_18a.text:
Morrow's expression sharpens. Just for a moment — a flicker of something harder underneath the control. "Where did you hear that name?"

You tell her. Drest, the old sailor in the Broken Wheel. She exhales through her nose. "Drest talks too much. He always has." She is quiet for a moment. Then: "Yes. The San Cristóbal. A Crown galleon, sunk ten years ago in the Shallows of Perdition. She was carrying gold and something else — a sealed pouch called the Red Correspondence. Whatever is in it, Ashford has killed to keep it buried."

She meets your eyes. "I know where the wreck is. I have known for three years. And I have not gone after it."

choices.text: Ask why she hasn't acted
Offer the Adamant for the salvage run

a_18b.text:
"Understood." Morrow sits back down. Her voice is level but something has closed behind her eyes. "Then we'll keep this professional. You've done me a service with the marine. I'll remember that."

She picks up her coffee. The meeting is over. You can feel it — the temperature in the room has dropped by several degrees.

Harlow opens the door for you. As you leave, she murmurs: "That was a mistake, Captain. She was offering you a seat at the table."

The harbour road stretches downhill toward the docks. The sun is past its peak. You have learned that Morrow is at the centre of things, but you have placed yourself at her edge.

choices.text: Return to the Broken Wheel
Find Aldous Crane — try the Council instead
Walk the docks and gather information independently

a_19.text:
Morrow is quiet for a long time. Long enough that Harlow shifts her weight by the door.

"Because diving that wreck requires a ship I'm willing to lose," she says finally. "The Shallows of Perdition earned their name. Reefs, currents, and Crown patrol routes that pass within sight. Any ship I send is a ship I can't use to defend this harbour."

She looks at the charts on her table. "And because the moment I move on the San Cristóbal, Ashford will know. He has eyes in this port — you just confirmed that. I have been sitting on the location because the act of going after it would tell him exactly where to look."

She folds her hands. "But the blockade changes the calculation. We're running out of time to wait."

choices.text: Offer the Adamant — your ship, your risk
Ask what she needs from you specifically

a_19a.text:
Morrow looks at you with what might be the first genuine expression you have seen from her. Not gratitude — assessment. She is calculating what your offer is worth, and whether you mean it.

"The Adamant. A sloop — small, fast, expendable." She says it without malice. It is simply true. "You would risk your ship on a salvage run into contested waters, on the word of a captain you met an hour ago."

She stands. "I don't need an answer now. Think about it. Talk to your crew — they deserve to know what you're considering." She pauses at the window. "Come back tomorrow. If you still want to do this, I'll give you what I know."

She has not said yes. But she has not said no. And she told you to consult your crew, which tells you something about the kind of captain she is.

choices.text: Return to the Adamant and talk to Pell
Head to the Broken Wheel — you need a drink first

a_19b.text:
"What I need," Morrow says, "is a captain who can think clearly under pressure and who doesn't have three years of Nassau politics weighing on every decision." She looks at you. "Someone the other factions haven't already measured and filed away."

She walks to the map on the wall. The Shallows of Perdition are marked in red ink, eight leagues south. "The wreck needs to be dived. The cargo needs to be recovered — all of it, the gold and the pouch. And it needs to happen before Ashford decides that waiting is no longer working and starts burning things."

She turns back to you. "Go think about it. Talk to your crew. You know where I am."

It is a dismissal, but a respectful one.

choices.text: Return to the Adamant
Visit the Broken Wheel first

a_20.text:
The Harbour Council's office is a converted warehouse at the east end of the dock — the only building on the waterfront with glass in its windows. A sign reads NASSAU FREE PORT AUTHORITY in letters that were once gold and are now mostly bare wood.

Inside, it is surprisingly orderly. Ledgers on shelves. A clerk copying figures. And at a desk near the window, a man in a pressed linen coat looks up from his work.

Aldous Crane. The Council's factor. He is perhaps fifty, with thinning hair combed precisely and spectacles that he removes when he sees you — a deliberate gesture, making sure you see his face clearly.

"The captain of the new sloop," he says. "I was wondering when you'd find your way here. Please — sit."

choices.text: Sit down and hear what he has to say
Stay standing — ask him what the Council wants with you

a_20a.text:
You cross the tavern to Crane's table. He watches you approach without looking up from his ledger, which is a particular kind of power play — the kind that says I know you're coming and I'm not impressed enough to stop working.

When you reach the table, he sets down his pen with surgical precision. "Captain." He gestures to the chair across from him. "I've been expecting someone from the new sloop. I assumed it would be a mate or a bosun first — captains usually send scouts. You came yourself."

He adjusts his spectacles. "That's either confidence or impatience. Sit down and I'll find out which."

choices.text: Sit and talk
Tell him you don't have time for games — what does the Council want?

a_21.text:
Crane pours you a glass of something from a bottle that has no label — rum, but smoother than anything you've had in months. He pours one for himself with the same precision he applies to everything.

"The Harbour Council represents the merchants of Nassau. Legitimate trade — or as legitimate as anything gets here. We fund the port's infrastructure, we manage provisions, and we keep the economy functional when the captains are busy playing at war."

He drinks. "The blockade is killing us. Slowly, but killing us. Provisions are running low. Ships can't trade. The merchantmen are getting nervous." He sets down his glass. "We need the blockade broken. And we are pragmatic about how that happens."

choices.text: Ask what he's proposing
Ask him about the San Cristóbal
Tell him the Adamant is available — for the right price

a_22.text:
Crane's eyebrows rise a fraction. "Direct. I appreciate that — most captains in this port have forgotten how to say what they mean." He closes his ledger and folds his hands on top of it.

"The Council wants stability. We don't care who provides it — Morrow, you, the Almighty Himself — as long as ships can move and trade can flow. The blockade has been in place for eleven days. We have provisions for perhaps three weeks. After that, this port eats itself."

He leans forward slightly. "Every new ship that enters this harbour is an asset or a liability. I'm here to determine which one yours is."

choices.text: Tell him the Adamant is an asset — ask what he needs
Ask what the Council knows about why Ashford is here

a_23.text:
"What I'm proposing is an arrangement." Crane opens a drawer and produces a folded document. "The Council can provision your ship. Fresh water, food, basic stores — at cost, not the gouging prices the market is charging. In return, we need your commitment."

He unfolds the document. It is a letter of marque — or Nassau's version of one. "Sign this, and you're a registered privateer of the Nassau Free Republic. It means you can't cut and run when the blockade lifts. It means your ship is part of this port's defence."

He meets your eyes. "It also means the Council backs you. Politically, financially. That has value in a place like this."

choices.text: Sign the letter — commit to Nassau
Ask for time to think about it
Refuse — you won't sign away the Adamant's freedom

a_24.text:
Crane goes very still. It is the first time you have seen him lose control of his expression — and it only lasts a heartbeat before he recovers.

"The San Cristóbal." He removes his spectacles and polishes them slowly. "Where did you hear that name?"

You tell him. He replaces his spectacles and considers you.

"The Council has old survey records. Pre-Republic, from when this was still a Crown port. The San Cristóbal was a treasury galleon — lost ten years ago in the Shallows of Perdition. We know she was carrying a war chest. Gold, silver, the usual." He pauses. "We also know Captain Morrow has the exact location. And she has been sitting on it for three years."

He taps the table once. "Ask yourself why a woman with that information and four armed ships has not gone after a fortune in gold."

choices.text: Ask Crane what he thinks the answer is
Tell him you'll find out yourself

a_24a.text:
Crane leans back in his chair. "Officially, Ashford is enforcing Crown authority over an unlicensed port. Unofficially —" He adjusts his spectacles. "I've been keeping records in this port for nine years. I've seen Crown patrols come and go. They burn, they blockade, they negotiate, they leave. This is different."

He opens a ledger and turns it toward you. Columns of dates and ship movements, meticulously recorded. "Ashford's patrol pattern does not match a standard blockade. He repositions his ships every two days — always keeping one in view of the Shallows of Perdition, eight leagues south. He's not just blocking the harbour. He's guarding something."

He closes the ledger. "There's a wreck down there. A Crown galleon called the San Cristóbal. She sank ten years ago. And whatever she was carrying, Ashford would rather starve this port than let anyone dive it."

choices.text: Ask what was on the San Cristóbal
Ask about the Council's proposal — you want to hear terms

a_24b.text:
"Gold, certainly. A treasury consignment for the Jamaica garrison — significant, but not extraordinary." Crane folds his hands. "But the survey records mention a sealed diplomatic pouch. Something called the Red Correspondence. I don't know what's in it. No one does — except perhaps Morrow, and she's not talking."

He pauses. "But I know this: Ashford was a post captain ten years ago, commanding the escort squadron that was supposed to protect the San Cristóbal. She sank on his watch. He was promoted afterward, which is — unusual, after losing a treasury ship."

He looks at you over his spectacles. "Whatever is in that pouch, it's the reason he's sitting three warships on our harbour. And it's the key to making him leave."

choices.text: Discuss the Council's terms — you're interested
Thank Crane and leave — you need to think

a_25.text:
Crane's mouth twitches — not quite a smile. "For the right price. A mercenary, then." He does not say it with contempt. He says it the way a man says "rain" when looking at clouds — a simple observation of conditions.

"The Council doesn't hire mercenaries, Captain. We invest in partners. The difference is that partners share the risk and share the reward. Mercenaries take their fee and leave." He opens his ledger. "I can offer you a better arrangement than a fee. But it requires commitment."

He produces a folded document from his drawer.

choices.text: Hear his terms
Tell him you're not interested in partnerships — just information

a_26.text:
You read the document. It is short — Crane is not a man who wastes words on paper any more than in person. The terms are clear: the Adamant is registered as a vessel of the Nassau Free Republic. You cannot leave port without Council authorisation while the blockade stands. In return, the Council provisions your ship and backs your interests in any dispute with other captains.

You sign. Crane blots the signature with a care that borders on reverence.

"Welcome to Nassau, Captain. Properly, this time." He stands and offers his hand. His grip is dry and precise. "I'll have provisions sent to your ship by evening. And I'll send word to the other Council members — they'll want to meet you."

He pauses at the door. "One more thing. Talk to Morrow. She has information this port needs. Tell her the Council is willing to deal."

choices.text: Head to the Broken Wheel to find Morrow's people
Return to the Adamant first

a_27.text:
Crane nods. "Take your time. But understand — time is the one thing this port is running out of." He returns to his ledger with a finality that says the conversation is over until you are ready to continue it.

The harbour road is bright after the dim interior. The blockade ships sit on the horizon, unchanged. Patient. You can feel the weight of what you've learned settling into your thinking — the San Cristóbal, the Red Correspondence, Morrow sitting on the location for three years.

The pieces of a puzzle, but not enough of them yet.

choices.text: Head to the Broken Wheel
Walk the docks — talk to the locals
Return to the Adamant
Check the far end of the dock — you noticed someone watching earlier

a_28.text:
Crane replaces the document in the drawer without visible emotion. "That is your right, Captain. The Adamant is your ship."

He picks up his pen. "But understand the arithmetic. Without Council backing, you provision at market rates — which are currently three times pre-blockade prices and rising. Your water casks are low. Your foremast needs work. You will burn through whatever reserves you have in a week."

He begins writing. "The door remains open. When the arithmetic changes your mind, you know where I am."

It is the politest dismissal you have ever received.

choices.text: Head to the Broken Wheel
Walk the docks
Return to the Adamant

a_30.text:
The docks of Nassau stretch in a rough crescent along the harbour. You walk the length of them, taking the measure of the place. Fishing boats hauled up on the shingle. Three brigs with their sails unbent, going nowhere. A careening yard where a sloop lies on her side, her hull black with weed.

The dockworkers move with the particular slowness of men who have nowhere to be and no cargo to shift. The blockade has frozen everything. A woman mending nets looks up at you. "New ship?" She does not wait for an answer. "Hope you brought food. We're eating hope and boiled leather up here."

choices.text: Ask her about the blockade
Keep walking — look for someone who knows more
Ask about other captains in port

a_31.text:
She works the needle through the net without looking up. "Eleven days. No trade. No fishing past the point — Crown frigates will take your boat and press your crew. Morrow says she's handling it. The Council says they're negotiating. Nobody's doing a damn thing that I can see."

She bites off a length of twine. "There's talk. Rumours. Some old wreck down south that's supposed to save us all." She shakes her head. "Same story every time. Some treasure, some secret, some miracle. Meanwhile, my children eat once a day."

She looks up at you for the first time. Her eyes are sharp and tired. "You want to help? Bring food. Everything else is just captains talking."

choices.text: Keep walking the docks
Head to the Broken Wheel — follow the rumour
Cut through the back alleys toward the tavern

a_32.text:
Further along the dock, past the chandler's shop with its empty shelves, you find a group of sailors sitting on upturned crates. They are from a brig called the Gallant — been stuck in port since the blockade dropped. Their captain is ashore somewhere, drinking.

One of them, a big man with a tattooed neck, looks at you sideways. "You're off the sloop that came in this morning. The Adamant." He stands. He is larger standing up. "You're in our berth."

This is not true — you can see the harbourmaster's chalk marks from here, and the Adamant is tied where the pilot put her. But the man does not care about chalk marks. He cares about the fact that his ship is going nowhere and yours just arrived.

choices.text: Point out the chalk marks — you're in your rightful berth
Offer to buy them a drink and sort this out
Tell him to take it up with the harbourmaster

a_33.text:
You ask the net-mender about the captains currently in port. She counts on her fingers.

"Morrow — the Tempest. She's the one everyone looks to. Then there's Garza on the Dorado, but his ship's falling apart. Captain Hatch on the Kingfisher — mean as a snake, that one. Stays out of politics."

She pauses. "There was a Captain Briggs on the Resolute. He tried to run the blockade four days ago. The Crown ships fired on him. He turned back, but he took damage. He's been in the Broken Wheel ever since, drinking and talking about how Morrow should have done more."

She shakes her head. "Morrow. Always Morrow. Like she's the only one with a brain in this port."

choices.text: Walk further along the docks
Head to the Broken Wheel
Ask about the old wreck she mentioned

a_33a.text:
She waves a dismissive hand. "The San Cristóbal. Crown ship that went down years ago, south of here. Some kind of treasure ship — gold, papers, the usual story." She threads her needle through the net. "Old Drest in the Broken Wheel talks about it every time someone buys him rum. Says he knew the captain. Says someone knows where it is."

She looks at you sharply. "If you're thinking about treasure hunting during a blockade, Captain, you're either brave or stupid. Probably both."

choices.text: Head to the Broken Wheel to find Drest
Keep walking the docks

a_34.text:
"Chalk marks." The big man looks at you like you have just quoted scripture in a brothel. "You hear that, lads? Chalk marks."

The other sailors laugh, but it is the thin, dangerous kind of laughter that comes before trouble. The big man steps closer. He smells of rum and unwashed canvas.

You hold your ground and point to the marks. Your hand does not shake. You state the facts: harbourmaster assigned the berth, the Adamant was piloted in by the port pilot, and the Gallant is tied fifty feet north of here in her own assigned spot.

The facts are boring. The man wanted a fight, and you gave him bureaucracy. After a long moment, he spits on the dock and sits back down. "Keep your berth," he mutters. "Won't matter when Ashford burns us all."

choices.text: Move on — check the far end of the dock
Head to the Broken Wheel

a_35.text:
The tension drops by one degree. Rum is the universal solvent in Nassau.

You walk with the Gallant's men to a drink-seller on the corner — a woman pouring from a barrel into tin cups. You buy a round. The big man's name is Cork, and he is less dangerous with a drink in his hand.

"We've been here three weeks," he says. "Captain Garza — the Dorado — he says Morrow's got a plan. But Morrow's always got a plan, and we're still sitting here." He drinks. "There's a story going around. Some wreck down south. Gold. The answer to everything." He shakes his head. "Fairy tales."

choices.text: Ask Cork what he knows about the wreck
Thank him and move on

a_35a.text:
Cork shrugs. "San Cristóbal. Crown treasury ship. Went down ten years back — the Shallows of Perdition, south of here. Reefs like razors, currents that'll pull a ship sideways." He finishes his drink. "Old Drest in the Broken Wheel says he knew her captain. Says someone in this port knows exactly where she sits. But nobody's going after her."

He looks at the blockade ships on the horizon. "Maybe because the man who lost her is the same man with three warships on our doorstep. Commodore Ashford. He was her escort captain when she went down. Got promoted afterward." He laughs bitterly. "Crown takes care of its own, even when they sink a fortune."

This is new. Ashford was directly connected to the San Cristóbal's loss.

choices.text: Head to the Broken Wheel to find Drest
Check the far end of the dock

a_36.text:
"Harbourmaster." The big man steps forward. His fists are the size of grapefruit. "The harbourmaster is a drunk who hasn't left the fort in four days. You want to hide behind him?"

The other Gallant sailors are standing now. This is going wrong quickly.

Your hand moves to the cutlass at your belt. The weight of it — familiar, solid. The big man sees the movement and pauses. He is angry, but he is not stupid. A dock fight with a captain is different from shoving around a deckhand.

choices.text: Draw the cutlass — make it clear you won't be pushed
Step back and defuse — this isn't worth blood

a_37.text:
Your cutlass clears the scabbard — a clean, practised draw. The blade catches the harbour light. It is not a threatening gesture. It is a statement of fact: you are armed, you are trained, and this conversation has reached its limit.

The big man looks at the blade. He looks at your face. He makes a calculation that ends with him sitting back down on his crate.

"Keep your berth," he says. But his eyes do not leave the cutlass until you sheathe it.

The other sailors settle. The moment passes. But it has been noted — by them, by the dockworkers who were watching, by the woman mending nets. The new captain off the Adamant is not to be pushed.

choices.text: Move on down the dock
Head to the Broken Wheel

a_38.text:
You take your hand off the cutlass and step back. Palms open. "I'm not here to fight over a berth," you say. "We're all stuck in the same harbour under the same blockade. Fighting each other is what Ashford wants."

The big man stares at you. He was ready for a fight and you took it away from him, which is its own kind of victory. After a long moment, he grunts and sits down.

"Fine. Keep your spot." He picks up his cup. "But when Ashford burns this harbour, your sloop burns with ours."

choices.text: Head to the far end of the dock
Go to the Broken Wheel

a_40.text:
The old sailor watches you approach with the particular wariness of a man who has been bought too many drinks with strings attached. His name, you will learn, is Drest. His hair is white, his teeth are broken, and his hands shake with a tremor that speaks of decades of rum and weather.

You set a cup in front of him. He looks at it. He looks at you.

"Buying my conversation or my silence?" he asks. His voice is rougher than the dock pilings.

choices.text: Your conversation — tell him you're new and want to understand this place
Ask him about the name you heard — San Cristóbal
Ask him what he knows about the blockade

a_41.text:
Drest drinks. He wipes his mouth with the back of his hand. "Understand this place. Right." He looks around the tavern — at Harlow by the bar, at Crane in his corner, at the men drinking at tables.

"Nassau is a republic. That's what they call it. What it actually is — it's a town full of people who can't go anywhere else, trying to pretend they chose to be here." He drinks again. "Morrow holds it together. The Council pays for it. And the Crown keeps trying to take it back."

He leans forward. "But this time it's different. This time Ashford isn't here to take the port. He's here because of what's under the water, eight leagues south."

He taps the table. "The San Cristóbal."

choices.text: Ask him about the San Cristóbal

a_42.text:
Drest's eyes sharpen. For a moment, the tremor in his hands stops. "The San Cristóbal. Crown treasury galleon. I sailed with her captain — Rodrigo de Vega. Good man. Careful man. She went down in the Shallows of Perdition ten years ago."

He rolls his cup between his palms. "She was carrying gold. A war chest for the Jamaica garrison. Fifty thousand pounds sterling, maybe more. And something else — a sealed diplomatic pouch. Red wax, Crown cipher. Nobody on the ship knew what was in it. Not even de Vega."

He looks at the door, as if checking who might be listening. "She had an escort squadron. Three ships, commanded by a post captain named Ashford." He lets the name hang in the air. "The same Ashford who's sitting on our harbour right now with three warships."

choices.text: Ask what happened — how did the San Cristóbal sink?
Ask if anyone knows where the wreck is

a_43.text:
Drest stares into his cup. "Storm. That's what the official report said. Storm in the Shallows of Perdition, ship driven onto the reefs. All hands lost." He pauses. "Except that's not what happened. I was on a trading sloop two leagues east that night. The weather was rough, but it wasn't a killing storm. Not for a ship that size."

He lowers his voice. "Ashford's escort squadron was supposed to be with her. They weren't. They'd broken formation twelve hours earlier — Ashford signalled that he was pursuing a pirate sail to the north. By the time the San Cristóbal hit the reefs, she was alone."

He meets your eyes. "Two hundred men drowned. Ashford got promoted. And nobody ever asked why his escort wasn't where it was supposed to be."

choices.text: Ask where the wreck is
Ask why Ashford would abandon his own convoy

a_43a.text:
"That," Drest says, "is the question. And the answer is in that diplomatic pouch." He finishes his drink and pushes the cup toward you — a clear request for a refill.

"The Red Correspondence. That's what the quartermaster called it. Red wax, red ribbon. Whatever's in those papers, Ashford has spent ten years making sure nobody reads them. He got promoted after the San Cristóbal went down. Post captain to commodore in three years." Drest's voice is bitter. "You don't get promoted for losing a treasury ship. Unless someone wants to keep you quiet."

He taps the table. "Someone knows where that wreck is. Someone in this port. And Ashford knows they know. That's why he's here."

choices.text: Ask who has the location

a_44.text:
Drest laughs — a dry, cracking sound. "If I knew where she was sitting, do you think I'd be drinking rum in this shit-hole tavern?" He shakes his head. "But I know who does. Everyone knows. They just don't say it out loud."

He glances across the room — at Harlow, standing at the bar. "Morrow. Captain Reyna Morrow. She found the location three years ago. Survey charts, dead reckoning, I don't know how. And she has sat on it ever since. Three years."

He holds up a trembling finger. "Three years with a fortune in gold and the key to Ashford's destruction sitting on the ocean floor. And she hasn't moved. Why?" He looks at you expectantly, as if you might have the answer.

"That's the question that keeps me awake, Captain. That's the question that matters."

choices.text: Seek out Morrow — through Harlow at the bar
Find Aldous Crane — the Council might know more
Leave the tavern and check the docks
Thank Drest and return to the Adamant to think

a_50.text:
The far end of the dock is quieter. A warehouse with a caved-in roof. A beached longboat with grass growing through its ribs. And a man leaning against a piling, watching the Adamant.

He is trying to look casual. He is failing. His boots are wrong — polished, square-toed, regulation Crown Navy issue. His coat is civilian, but it fits the way a uniform fits — squared at the shoulders. He has a knife at his belt but no pistol visible.

He has been watching your ship. You can see it in his sightline — he has positioned himself with a clear view of the Adamant's deck and the dock approach.

He has not noticed you yet.

choices.text: Approach him openly
Circle around and come at him from behind
Watch him from a distance — see what he does

a_51.text:
You walk toward him directly. He sees you when you are twenty paces out, and you watch the calculation happen behind his eyes — stay, run, or fight. He chooses to stay, which means he has orders or he is stupid. He does not look stupid.

"Afternoon," you say. "You seem very interested in my ship."

His jaw tightens. "Your ship? I'm just watching the harbour." His accent is Home Counties English — educated, clipped. Not a common sailor. An officer in plainclothes.

"You're watching the Adamant specifically," you say. "You've been here long enough to leave boot prints in the mud. Crown marine, I'd guess. Sent in with the morning tide to watch new arrivals."

choices.text: Press him — who sent him and why
Offer him a way out — tell him you're not looking for trouble

a_52.text:
You take the long way around — through the warehouse with the caved-in roof, stepping carefully over broken planks and ship's debris. The marine does not hear you. He is focused on the Adamant.

You emerge behind him, six feet away. Close enough to see the Crown anchor tattooed on the back of his neck, normally hidden by a uniform collar.

You clear your throat.

He spins, and his hand goes to his knife. But you are already standing with your arms folded, and the moment is yours.

"You're watching my ship," you say. "I'd like to know why."

choices.text: Keep the pressure on — demand answers
Ease off — you just want information

a_53.text:
You find a spot behind the beached longboat and watch. The marine does not move much. He checks a pocket watch — Crown issue, by the look of it. He makes notes in a small book. He watches the Adamant, then scans the harbour, then watches the Adamant again.

After fifteen minutes, he straightens up. He looks toward the harbour mouth — toward the blockade ships — and makes a hand signal. You follow his gaze. A longboat is pulling away from the Crown's Justice, too far out to read details, but the timing is not coincidental.

He is signalling the flagship. Reporting on new arrivals. This is not casual surveillance — it is structured intelligence gathering.

He turns to leave.

choices.text: Step out and confront him before he goes
Follow him — see where he goes
Let him go — you've seen enough

a_53a.text:
You shadow him along the waterfront. He walks with purpose — not running, but not dawdling. He passes the market, cuts through an alley between two warehouses, and emerges at a small cove north of the main harbour.

A boat is waiting — a ship's launch with two oarsmen. Crown sailors, trying to look like fishermen and failing at it the same way their officer failed to look like a civilian.

The marine climbs into the boat. One of the oarsmen hands him a folded paper. He reads it, nods, and tucks it inside his coat. The oarsmen begin pulling for the harbour mouth — and the blockade ships beyond.

He is reporting to Ashford directly. Every new ship that enters Nassau is being catalogued and assessed.

choices.text: Return to report this to Harlow at the Broken Wheel
Return to the Broken Wheel

a_54.text:
"Who sent you?" Your hand rests on the hilt of your cutlass. The marine's eyes track the movement.

"I don't answer to pirates," he says. But his voice is not steady. He is alone on a hostile dock, and he knows it.

"You're standing on a Nassau dock watching a Nassau ship. That makes this a Nassau matter." You step closer. "Ashford sent you. To watch new arrivals. To report back which ships might be a threat to the blockade."

His silence confirms it. His hand moves toward his knife.

choices.text: Draw your cutlass — end this quickly
Back off — you have what you need

a_54a.text:
The cutlass comes out clean. The marine draws his knife — a naval boarding knife, heavy and well-maintained. He drops into a fighting stance that confirms every suspicion: this man has trained with blade and cutlass since he was a midshipman.

"This is a mistake," he says through his teeth. "Kill a Crown marine and Ashford will have his justification. He'll burn this port to the waterline."

a_54b.text:
The marine goes down on one knee, blood running from a cut on his arm. His knife clatters on the dock. He looks up at you with a mix of pain and professional respect.

"Finish it, then," he says. But his eyes say something else. They say: I am a dead man either way. If you let me go, Ashford will know I was compromised. If you kill me, Ashford has his excuse to attack.

A dead Crown marine on the dock is a problem. A live one who owes you his life might be something else.

choices.text: Let him go — tell him to report that Nassau is watching too
Take his notebook and send him back with a message for Ashford

a_55.text:
You raise your hands slightly. "I'm not looking for a fight. I just want to know what a Crown officer is doing watching my ship."

The marine relaxes — a fraction. "Commodore Ashford's orders. Every ship that enters or leaves the harbour is logged. Crew complement, armament, captain's name if we can get it." He pauses, as if deciding how much to say. "It's reconnaissance. Standard blockade procedure."

"And what have you logged about the Adamant?"

He almost smiles. "Sloop. Single mast. Ten guns, maybe twelve. Small crew. Not a threat." He looks at you. "No offence meant."

It is strange to hear your ship dismissed so casually. And useful. If Ashford considers the Adamant beneath notice, that is an advantage.

choices.text: Ask him why Ashford is really here — what's he after?
Let him go and report what you've learned

a_55a.text:
You step back. The marine does not relax, but his hand moves away from his knife. The moment stretches — two men on a dock, neither willing to turn their back on the other.

"I'm going," the marine says. "You've made your point." He begins backing toward the north end of the dock, eyes on you.

You let him go. He has confirmed what you suspected: Ashford has eyes inside the harbour. Every new arrival is being watched, measured, reported. The Adamant has been noted.

The question is whether that is a disadvantage or an opportunity.

choices.text: Report to Harlow at the Broken Wheel
Head to the Broken Wheel

a_55b.text:
You sheathe your cutlass and step back. "Go. Tell Ashford that Nassau sees him too. Every longboat, every signal flag, every marine in bad civilian clothes."

The marine picks up his knife, sheathes it, and stands. He meets your eyes — a long look, the kind that happens between professionals. Then he nods, once, and walks toward the north end of the dock.

You watch him go. A Crown marine, beaten and released. He will report back. Ashford will know that his surveillance has been compromised. That changes the game.

Your cutlass goes back in the scabbard. There is blood on the dock. Not much, but enough to darken the wood.

choices.text: Report to Harlow at the Broken Wheel
Head to the Broken Wheel

a_55c.text:
You pick up his notebook. It is Crown-issue — bound in oilcloth, filled with neat handwriting. Ship names, crew estimates, armament assessments. Pages of intelligence, gathered over eleven days.

"Tell Ashford this," you say. "His intelligence operation in this harbour is over. And his marine was beaten by the captain of a ship he considered beneath notice."

The marine takes a long breath. "You're making an enemy, Captain."

"I already have one. He's got three warships." You gesture toward the harbour mouth. "Go."

He goes. You tuck the notebook inside your coat. This is worth more than a sword — it is a map of everything Ashford knows and everything he does not.

choices.text: Report to Harlow at the Broken Wheel
Head to the Broken Wheel

a_55d.text:
The marine hesitates. Then something shifts in his expression — not defection, but the particular exhaustion of a man who has been following orders he does not fully understand.

"Ashford is looking for something. A wreck. He won't say what's on it, but we've been told to watch for any ship preparing a salvage operation — diving equipment, extra rope, that sort of thing." He pauses. "He's scared, Captain. I've served under him for four years, and I have never seen him scared before. Whatever is down there, it can hurt him."

He straightens up. "That's all I'm saying. I need to go."

He walks away quickly, not looking back.

choices.text: Head to the Broken Wheel and share what you've learned

a_56.text:
The Broken Wheel is busier now — the afternoon crowd. You find a spot at the bar and take stock of what you know.

Ashford is afraid. The blockade is not about Nassau — it is about the San Cristóbal. Whatever is in the Red Correspondence, it can destroy him. Morrow knows where the wreck is and has not acted. The Council is running out of time and patience.

And you — a captain with a small, fast sloop that nobody considers a threat — are sitting in the middle of all of it.

choices.text: Approach Harlow — you have information Morrow needs
Talk to Aldous Crane
Find Drest — the old sailor who knows about the San Cristóbal
Return to the Adamant

a_60.text:
The Adamant rocks gently at her berth. The familiar creak of timber and hemp — after a day in Nassau, it is the most honest sound you have heard. Pell is on deck, mending a line with quick, practised hands. He looks up as you come aboard.

"You look like a man who's been thinking too hard," he says. It is not a question.

choices.text: Tell Pell everything you've learned — the San Cristóbal, the blockade, Ashford
Tell Pell the basics — blockade, tensions, political factions — but keep the San Cristóbal to yourself for now
Ask Pell how the crew is doing

a_61.text:
You sit on a coil of rope and tell Pell everything. The San Cristóbal. The Red Correspondence. Ashford's personal stake. Morrow sitting on the location. The Council running out of time.

Pell listens without interrupting — a habit he learned years ago, when he realised that captains who talk need an audience, not advice. When you finish, he is quiet for a while.

"So you're thinking about diving a wreck in contested waters to recover papers that could destroy a commodore." He ties off his line. "And the only person who knows where the wreck is won't tell you yet."

He looks at you. "What does the crew get out of this, Captain?"

It is the right question. Pell always asks the right question.

choices.text: Tell him the crew gets a share of the gold — and a port that's free to sail from
Tell him you don't know yet — you're still working it out

a_62.text:
You give Pell the broad strokes. The blockade is political, not military. There are factions — Morrow, the Council. Everyone is watching the new arrival.

Pell nods slowly. He is reading your face as much as listening to your words. He knows you are holding something back. He knows you know he knows.

"All right," he says. "You'll tell me the rest when you're ready." No accusation. No resentment. Just the patient certainty of a man who has sailed with you long enough to know your patterns.

Tomas comes up from below. "The crew's asking about provisions, Captain. Market prices are triple. The lads are nervous."

choices.text: Tell Pell to draw from the ship's purse — the crew eats properly
Tell them to make do for now — you'll sort it tomorrow

a_63.text:
Pell sets down his line. "Nervous. They've been watching those warships all day. Tomas has been below talking about press gangs — you know how he gets. Jennings is keeping quiet, which is worse."

He lowers his voice. "They need to know we have a plan, Captain. Or at least that you're working on one. A crew that doesn't know what's coming starts inventing things to be afraid of."

From below deck, you can hear voices — low, tense, the particular murmur of men who are worried and trying not to show it.

choices.text: Go below and address the crew directly
Tell Pell to reassure them — you'll have a plan by morning

a_63a.text:
"Draw from the ship's purse. Whatever it costs, the crew eats properly tonight." Pell raises an eyebrow — the prices will hurt — but he nods. "Aye, Captain."

He calls for Tomas. The orders go out. You can see the crew's mood shift as the word spreads — not dramatically, not with cheers or gratitude. Just a loosening. Their captain is looking after them in a hostile port. It is noted.

That night, the Adamant smells of cooked food and the crew's voices carry across the harbour — not loud, but alive. In a port full of ships going nowhere, it is a small defiance.

choices.text: Turn in for the night — tomorrow the real work begins

a_63b.text:
The crew — eight men in total, not counting Pell — are gathered in the hold. Tomas, the youngest, is talking. He stops when he sees you on the ladder.

You give it to them straight. Nassau is under blockade. The situation is serious but not hopeless. You are meeting with the local captains and the port authority. You will have a plan within a day.

You do not mention the San Cristóbal. Not yet. But you tell them this: "We came here for a reason. We're staying for a reason. And whatever happens, the Adamant's crew comes first."

The silence that follows is not uncomfortable. It is the silence of men deciding whether to believe you. Pell, standing behind you, says nothing. His presence there says enough.

choices.text: Head back topside

a_64.text:
"The gold gets split. Standard shares — crew gets their portion, same as any prize. And when the blockade breaks, the Adamant sails free from a port that knows our name."

Pell considers this. "Standard shares. And the papers?"

"The papers are leverage. They break Ashford. No more blockade, no more Crown breathing down this port's neck. That's worth more than gold to the people here — and to us, if we plan to stay."

Pell is quiet for a moment. Then he nods. "Tell me when. I'll have the crew ready."

It is not loyalty. It is something more practical — trust, built on years of shared risk and fair dealing.

choices.text: Turn in for the night

a_64a.text:
"I don't know yet." You say it plainly, without apology. Pell watches you for a moment.

"That's honest, at least." He picks up his line and starts working the splice again. "The crew can handle uncertainty, Captain. What they can't handle is being lied to. Keep telling them the truth — even when the truth is 'I don't know' — and they'll follow you."

He ties off the splice. "But don't take too long figuring it out. This port is a powder keg and those warships are the match."

The sun is going down. The blockade ships are black shapes against an orange sky.

choices.text: Turn in for the night

a_65.text:
The evening settles over Nassau like a damp blanket. Lanterns appear along the harbour road. The sound of the Broken Wheel — music, raised voices, something breaking — carries across the water.

You stand on the Adamant's deck and look at the three ships on the horizon. Their riding lights are lit — steady points of light that do not move. Crown's Justice. Fervent. Resolve. They have been there for eleven days, and they will be there tomorrow.

Pell comes up from below. "Crew's settled. Jennings has the watch." He stands beside you, looking at the same lights. "Hell of a place, Captain."

"Hell of a time," you say.

choices.text: Turn in for the night

a_70.text:
You sleep in the captain's cabin — a space barely wider than the bunk it contains, smelling of salt and old wood. The Adamant creaks around you in the darkness. Through the stern window, the blockade ships' lights are visible, unblinking.

You dream of nothing. You wake before dawn.

When you come on deck, the harbour is grey and still. Pell is already up, coffee in hand — real coffee, sourced from somewhere.

"Visitors," he says, nodding toward the dock.

A boy is standing on the wharf, shifting his weight from foot to foot. He holds a folded note.

choices.text: Take the note

a_71.text:
The note is short. No signature. The handwriting is precise and unhurried.

"Captain — the situation has changed overnight. A fourth Crown ship has been sighted to the east. Ashford is tightening the noose. If you are serious about what we discussed, come to the Broken Wheel at noon. Bring your chart."

The boy waits. "Any reply?" he asks.

You look at Pell. He looks at the note. He looks at the horizon, where — yes — there is a new shape among the warships. A fourth sail.

"That changes things," Pell says quietly.

choices.text: Tell the boy: 'I'll be there'
Ask the boy who sent the note

a_71a.text:
The boy shrugs with the particular blankness of someone who has been paid not to answer questions. "Got a penny for it, sir. Lady at the Wheel gave it to me." He holds out his hand for a reply or a coin, whichever comes first.

Harlow. Or Morrow herself. Either way, the invitation is clear — and the urgency is real. A fourth Crown ship means the blockade is not a holding pattern. It is a tightening grip.

Pell has his arms crossed. "Your call, Captain."

choices.text: Tell the boy you'll be there at noon

a_72.text:
The boy runs off. You watch him disappear into the harbour crowd.

Noon is hours away. The morning stretches before you — time to prepare. To think. To decide what kind of captain you are going to be in this place.

The fourth ship sits on the horizon. The harbour is waking up. You can hear the market opening — the scrape of crates, the calls of sellers. Nassau, going through the motions of a normal morning while the noose tightens.

You check your cutlass. You check your chart. You drink the rest of Pell's coffee.

Nassau is running out of time. And somehow, impossibly, the captain of a sloop that nobody considers a threat might be the one who changes that.

choices.text: Check in with the crew before you go
Check in with the crew one last time
Head to the Broken Wheel early — get the lay of the land

a_73.text:
You find the crew on deck, watching the new ship on the horizon. Tomas has the glass and is trying to read her name. Jennings is sitting on the capstan, sharpening a knife with slow, deliberate strokes.

"Four ships now," Tomas says. He does not look away from the glass. "Captain, are we going to be all right?"

The question hangs in the salt air. You tell them what you can — that you are meeting with people who have a plan, that the Adamant has a role to play, that you will not leave them in the dark.

They listen. They nod. But you can see it in their faces — they are waiting for something more. Something you have not yet earned the right to give them.

Confidence. Trust.

choices.text: Head to the Broken Wheel

a_73a.text:
You find the crew on deck, watching the new ship. They are quieter than yesterday — not with fear, but with the focused stillness of men who know something is coming and trust their captain to meet it.

Pell has told them what he knows. They have questions — practical ones, not panicked ones. Where will the Adamant be positioned? How much warning will they have? What are the rules of engagement?

You answer what you can. For the rest, you tell them the truth: you are working on it.

Tomas nods. Jennings sheathes his knife and stands. "We're ready when you are, Captain," he says. It is the first time Jennings has spoken to you directly since you arrived in Nassau.

They trust you. Not blindly — that would be worse. They trust you because you have earned it.

choices.text: Head to the Broken Wheel

a_74.text:
The Broken Wheel at noon. The light comes through the shutters in slats, cutting the pipe smoke into layers. The room is half-full — captains, merchants, dockworkers. The mood is tighter than yesterday. Everyone has seen the fourth ship.

Harlow stands by the door. She nods when she sees you — the briefest acknowledgment. Crane is at his usual table, but his ledger is closed. Drest is absent.

A chair has been left empty at a table in the back. A cup of coffee sits in front of it, still steaming. The chair faces the door.

Someone is expecting you.

choices.text: Sit down

a_75.text:
You sit. The coffee is good — real, strong, the same kind Morrow was drinking yesterday. The chair faces the door, which means whoever placed it wanted you to see everyone who enters.

Morrow comes in two minutes later. She crosses the room without acknowledging anyone — not rude, just focused. She sits across from you and places a folded chart on the table.

"A fourth ship. The Reliance — thirty-six guns, frigate class. Ashford has called in reinforcements." She speaks quietly, but her voice carries the weight of someone who has stopped pretending there is time to wait.

"I have been sitting on the San Cristóbal's location for three years. That ends today." She unfolds the chart. "Here is what I need from you."

choices.text: Listen

a_76.text:
The chart shows the Shallows of Perdition — a maze of reef systems and sandbars eight leagues south of Nassau. Morrow has marked a spot with a small red cross.

"The San Cristóbal sits in twelve fathoms of water, here. The reef protects her from the open ocean but makes approach difficult — you need a shallow-draft vessel and a captain who can navigate tight channels." She looks at you. "A sloop."

She folds the chart. "The gold funds Nassau's defence. But the Red Correspondence is what matters. Whatever is in that pouch, it is the reason Ashford is here. It is the weapon that makes him leave."

She pushes the chart across the table. "Your ship. Your crew. Your risk. But this is not a request — it is an offer. Everything Nassau has rides on what comes out of that wreck."

The chart sits between you. The weight of it — paper and ink and the location of a ship that could change everything.

test_luck.lucky_text: You feel the familiar quickening — the sense that the tide is turning in your favour. The chart is clear, the markings precise. You can read the reef channels. The Adamant can make this run.
test_luck.unlucky_text: A cold feeling settles in your stomach. The chart is complex — the reef channels are narrow and the currents are marked in red. This will not be easy. The Adamant will be tested.

a_77.text:
You take the chart. The channels are navigable — tight, but the Adamant was built for tight channels. You can see the approach, the anchorage, the dive point. This is doable.

Morrow watches you read the chart. Something in her expression shifts — not relief, but the cautious hope of a woman who has been carrying this burden alone for three years.

"Act 2 begins at dawn," she says. "Be ready."

You fold the chart and tuck it inside your coat. The Broken Wheel carries on around you — talk, smoke, the clink of cups. Nassau does not know yet that the game has changed.

But you know. And Morrow knows. And somewhere on the horizon, aboard the Crown's Justice, Commodore Ashford does not.

Not yet.

a_77a.text:
You take the chart. The channels are worse than you expected — razor reefs, cross-currents marked in red ink, a notation in Morrow's hand that reads "tidal window: 2 hours." The Adamant can make the run, but there is no room for error.

Morrow sees your expression. "I never said it would be easy. I said it would be necessary."

She stands. "Dawn. Be ready. And Captain —" She pauses at the door. "Talk to your crew. They deserve to know what you're asking of them."

You fold the chart and sit alone in the Broken Wheel. The fourth ship sits on the horizon with its sisters. Four warships. One sloop. A wreck full of secrets.

Nassau's clock is ticking. And when dawn comes, the Adamant sails south.

a_80.text:
The harbour thug comes at you from behind a stack of crates — a dockside predator who has been watching you since you left the Broken Wheel. He is wiry, quick, and armed with a belaying pin that he swings at your head without preamble.

"Your purse, Captain. And that pretty cutlass while you're at it."

a_81.text:
The thug drops his belaying pin and scrambles backward, blood running from his nose. He is not brave enough to continue when the odds have turned. He disappears into the alley between the warehouses, leaving nothing behind but a few drops of blood on the cobblestones.

You check your coat. Everything is where it should be. The encounter was quick and ugly — a reminder that Nassau is not just politics and parleys. It is a port full of desperate people, and desperate people do desperate things.

Your cutlass goes back in the scabbard. Your hands are steady.

choices.text: Continue to your destination

a_82.text:
The market runs along the waterfront in a ragged line of blankets, barrels, and makeshift stalls. The selection tells you everything about Nassau's situation: ship's biscuit selling at four times the normal rate. Salt pork that has been turned too many times. Oranges — actual fresh oranges — going for prices that would make a London merchant blush.

A woman selling rope notices you looking. "Welcome to paradise, Captain. Everything's for sale, and nothing's worth buying."

A boy runs past carrying a message. A man argues with a chandler over the price of nails. The ordinary life of a port, stretched thin by circumstance.

choices.text: Ask the rope seller about the mood in the port
Move on to the Broken Wheel
Head back to the docks

a_83.text:
"Mood?" She coils a length of hemp with practised hands. "We're scared, Captain. That's the mood. Eleven days of warships on the horizon and nobody knows what Ashford wants. Morrow says stay calm. The Council says they're working on it. But nobody's eating any better and nobody's sailing anywhere."

She ties off the coil and sets it on the blanket. "I've got two children and a mother who can't work. If this port falls — to the Crown, to starvation, to whatever comes next — we've got nothing. No ship. No passage out. Just these docks and whatever comes down on them."

She looks at the harbour. "If you're here to help, help. If you're here to make money, get in line. And if you're just passing through —" She shrugs. "Good luck getting past those ships."

choices.text: Head to the Broken Wheel
Walk the docks

a_84.text:
The fort overlooks the harbour from the hill — a squat stone building with walls three feet thick and cannon muzzles poking through embrasures that have not been maintained in years. A Nassau flag — black, with no device — hangs limp from a pole on the rampart.

Two men sit outside the gate, playing cards on a barrel. They are nominally guards. They look at you with mild interest.

"New captain. The sloop." One of them gestures up the hill. "Harbourmaster's inside if you need him. He might be awake."

choices.text: Go inside and find the harbourmaster
Ask the guards what they can see from up here
Head back down to the harbour

a_85.text:
The harbourmaster is a heavy man named Coyle, asleep in a chair with his boots on a desk covered in papers. He wakes when you enter, blinking.

"Captain of what?" He listens to your introduction with the glazed attention of a man who has been drinking since breakfast. "The Adamant. Sloop. Right. I have you in berth seven. Pilot put you there."

He rummages through papers. "Harbour fee is two shillings a day. You can pay me or you can pay Crane at the Council office — he keeps better records anyway." He yawns. "Welcome to Nassau. Don't start any fires."

Useless, mostly. But you notice the charts on his wall — old ones, pre-Republic. Survey charts of the coastline south of Nassau. The Shallows of Perdition are clearly marked.

choices.text: Ask about the survey charts on the wall
Pay the fee and leave

a_85a.text:
Coyle follows your gaze. "Those? Crown survey charts. From before we took the port. Admiralty cartography — best in the world, say what you like about the bastards." He pulls one down from the wall. "Shallows of Perdition. Nasty piece of water, eight leagues south. Reefs, currents, sandbars that shift with the season."

He spreads the chart on the desk. "Ships have been going down there for a hundred years. The reefs are uncharted — every captain who tried to map them either gave up or added to the collection on the bottom."

He taps a spot on the chart. "Crown galleon went down there ten years ago. The San Cristóbal. Treasury ship." He looks at you. "Why? You planning a salvage run during a blockade?"

He laughs at his own joke. You do not.

choices.text: Ask him if anyone has ever dived the wreck
Thank him and leave

a_85b.text:
"Dived it? In those waters?" Coyle shakes his head. "You'd need a shallow-draft vessel — something that can thread the reef channels. A sloop, maybe. And you'd need to know the exact position, because the Shallows stretch for leagues and the currents will pull you sideways."

He replaces the chart on the wall. "Morrow asked me about these charts three years ago. Same questions you're asking. I gave her what I had. She never told me what she did with the information."

He sits back down and puts his boots on the desk. "If you're thinking what I think you're thinking, Captain — talk to Morrow. She's the one with the answers. I'm just the man with the old maps."

He closes his eyes. The conversation is over.

choices.text: Head to the Broken Wheel to find Morrow's people
Return to the docks

a_86.text:
One of the guards stands and walks you to the rampart. From here, you can see the entire harbour — every ship, every dock, every building. And beyond the harbour mouth, the blockade.

"Four ships now," the guard says. "New one came in at dawn. Frigate class." He points. "Crown's Justice is the big one — sixty guns, commodore's flag. Fervent and Resolve are the frigates on the flanks. The new one — haven't got her name yet — is sitting further east. Covering the Shallows."

Covering the Shallows. That detail lands with weight. Ashford has positioned his new ship specifically to watch the approach to the Shallows of Perdition.

"He's not just blocking the harbour," the guard says. "He's guarding something down there."

choices.text: Ask what's in the Shallows
Thank him and head back down

a_86a.text:
The guard shrugs. "Reefs, mostly. Bad water. Ships have gone down there for years." He pauses. "There's talk about a Crown wreck — the San Cristóbal. Treasury galleon. Old Drest down at the Broken Wheel talks about it when he's drunk, which is always."

He looks at the blockade ships. "But if Ashford's positioning a frigate to watch those waters specifically, maybe Drest isn't just talking. Maybe there's something down there worth guarding."

He sits back down and picks up his cards. "Above my pay grade, Captain. I just watch the horizon and play cards."

choices.text: Head to the Broken Wheel
Return to the docks
